ocr.text: In the following analysis, we present a comprehensive overview of the data concerning the detection of Operational Taxonomic
Units (OTUs) across various groups, offering insights into the temporal dynamics of lung cancer screening methodologies.
Number of OTUs expected by  Percentage of sampled OTUs from the expected number by
Groups  N  Number of OTUs detected  Chao approach  Jackknife approach  Bootstrapping approach  Chao approach  Jackknife approach  Bootstrapping approach
The table delineates the number of OTUs detected, which serves as a critical metric for assessing the diversity of microbial
communities associated with different screening intervals.
Considerations  Recurrent cross-sectional analysis  Trajectory analysis
Research focus  Describe the differences between time points  Describe how process or experience changes over time
Sample considerations  The cohort at each time point may be the same or differentMay be preferred if sample is highly transient or has high mortality over study duration  Must maintain same cohort

Theoretical approach  Determined by the research questionAny analytic approach may be used consistently throughout the study  Determined by the research questionAny analytic approach may be used consistently throughout the study
Level of data analysis  Whole sample (or subsamples)  Individual people or individual groups (e.g., families)
Timing of analysis  May analyze as each time point is completed  Must wait until data is collected at all time points
Notably, the Chao and Jackknife approaches are employed to estimate species richness, providing a robust framework for
understanding the variations in microbial profiles over time.
LC Stage at Diagnosis  Baseline Screening(LCs, n = 13)  Annual Screening After Baseline(LCs, n = 21)  All Screens(LCs, n = 34)
Indeterminate, n (%)*  1 (8)  1 (5)  2 (6)
By comparing baseline screening results from lung cancer patients (LCs, n = 13) to those observed during annual screenings
(LCs, n = 21), we aim to elucidate the impact of longitudinal assessment on microbial diversity. This analysis is pivotal in
informing future research directions and clinical practices, as it highlights the importance of recurrent cross-sectional analysis
and trajectory analysis in identifying significant changes in microbial communities. Ultimately, these findings contribute to a
nuanced understanding of how screening processes influence patient outcomes and the overall trajectory of disease
management.
